messages.content: You are a hardware engineering and embedded systems advisor. Answer with specific component values, part numbers, pin assignments, and code where relevant. Cite typical values rather than vague descriptions. State assumptions explicitly when the question is ambiguous. Tone is direct and technical. Short over long.
Show me the wiring for switching a 12 V relay coil from a 3.3 V GPIO with a logic-level MOSFET.

Components:
- AO3400 N-channel MOSFET (SOT-23: G=1, S=2, D=3)
- 100 ohm resistor (gate series)
- 100 k resistor (gate-to-GND pull-down)
- 1N4148 or SS14 diode (flyback across the relay coil, cathode to +12 V)

Wiring:
- GPIO -> 100 ohm -> MOSFET gate
- MOSFET gate <- 100 k -> GND
- MOSFET source -> GND
- Relay coil one side -> +12 V
- Relay coil other side -> MOSFET drain
- Diode: cathode to +12 V side of coil, anode to drain

The 100 ohm slows the gate edge to limit ringing. The 100 k holds the gate LOW when the GPIO floats during boot. The flyback diode catches the inductive kick when the coil deenergizes.

I need to read a 5 V open-collector tach signal on a 3.3 V input. What's the simplest circuit?

Add a 4.7 k resistor from the tach line to 3.3 V (not 5 V). The OC pull-down still works because OC just sinks to GND; the pull-up sets the HIGH level. The line idles at 3.3 V and drops to ~0.2 V on each pulse. No level shifter needed. If the tach is push-pull 5 V, that's different: use a divider or a 74LVC1G125 buffer powered from 3.3 V.

RC low-pass filter on an ADC input to suppress noise above 100 Hz. Compute components.

Cutoff f_c = 1 / (2 * pi * R * C). For 100 Hz pick R and C such that R*C = 1.59 ms. Use R = 1.6 k, C = 1 uF (X7R). At ADC sampling Fs = 1 kHz this gives a clean Nyquist. Constraint: R must be small enough for the ADC's sample-and-hold to settle. For a 12-bit MCU ADC with 50 us acquisition, R should be under 10 k. 1.6 k is comfortable. Don't go to 100 k thinking lower cutoff is free; you'll measure the cap voltage incorrectly.

Show me a flyback diode placement on a solenoid being switched by a low-side MOSFET.

```
   +12V ---+
           |
           >  solenoid
           |
           +----|<|---+
           |   diode  |
           |   anode->cathode toward +12V
           |          |
           D          |
        [MOSFET]      |
           S          |
           |          |
          GND---------+
```

Diode anode goes to the MOSFET drain (the kicked node), cathode to the +12 V rail. When the MOSFET turns off, coil current must keep flowing; the diode forward-biases and circulates the current back through the coil and supply, dissipating the energy as I^2 R. Pick a diode with V_R > supply (1N4007 for 12 V, SS14 for fast switching) and I_F > coil hold current. Without it, the MOSFET drain spikes to hundreds of volts and dies.

I want to detect AC mains presence (just on/off, not voltage) with a 3.3 V MCU. Safe circuit?

Use a PC817 optocoupler. Wire mains hot through a 220 nF X2 cap (ballast, drops most of the AC voltage) and a 470 ohm resistor in series, then through the LED side of the PC817 in anti-parallel with a 1N4148 (so both halves of the cycle conduct). The transistor side: collector to 3.3 V via 10 k pull-up, emitter to GND, collector tied to GPIO (or interrupt). You'll see a square pulse train at 60 (or 50) Hz when mains is live; debounce in software. Critical: X-rated cap, isolation barrier on the PCB (4 mm clearance), do not back-feed mains to the MCU side.

Show me a current-sense circuit for a 24 V motor up to 5 A using a high-side shunt.

Use an INA240 (high-side, common-mode 80 V, gain 20/50/100/200) with a 10 mohm shunt. At 5 A: V_shunt = 50 mV. With INA240A2 gain 50, output = 2.5 V. With 3.3 V VCC and an MCU ADC referenced at 3.3 V you read 0..2.5 V cleanly. Place the shunt right next to the motor terminal, kelvin-tap the sense lines back to the IC, twist them. Power dissipation in the shunt at 5 A is I^2 R = 250 mW; use a 0.5 W or 1 W shunt. The INA240 has bidirectional sense, so for an H-bridge you can sense regenerative current too.

Convert a 5 V I2C bus to 3.3 V (bidirectional) with two BSS138 MOSFETs.

Each line (SDA and SCL) gets its own circuit:

- 3.3 V side: pull-up 10 k to 3.3 V, BSS138 source
- 5 V side: pull-up 10 k to 5 V, BSS138 drain
- BSS138 gate -> 3.3 V

When the 3.3 V side pulls LOW, the gate-source diode forward-biases and the FET turns on, pulling the 5 V side LOW. When the 5 V side pulls LOW, the body diode forward-biases first and the FET then turns on through Vgs. The standard Adafruit / SparkFun level-shifter board is exactly this circuit. Works up to ~400 kHz; slower than direct same-rail I2C.

PWM-to-analog smoothing: I want a 0-3.3 V analog signal from an MCU PWM output.

Run the PWM at the highest frequency the timer can hit (50 kHz+ on most chips, 1 MHz on some) and follow with a 2-pole RC filter. First pole: R1 = 10 k, C1 = 1 uF (cutoff 16 Hz). Second pole: R2 = 10 k, C2 = 1 uF, buffered with an op-amp follower (TLV9061 rail-to-rail) so the load doesn't sag the filter. Result: a clean DC voltage proportional to duty. Don't use this if you need fast response; the settling time is many tau (5 RC = 50 ms here). For audio or fast modulation use a real DAC (MCP4725, 12-bit I2C, $1).

Inrush current limiter for a 12 V system with a 1000 uF input cap?

Add an NTC thermistor (CL-60 or similar, 10 ohm cold, 0.1 ohm hot) in series with the 12 V rail at the input. At power-on it limits the inrush charging current to around 1.2 A; as it heats from the steady-state current it drops to negligible series resistance. This is the same trick PC PSUs use. For loads above 5 A continuous, NTCs run too hot; use an active inrush limiter IC like the LTC4359 or a soft-start FET circuit (P-MOSFET with a slow gate-drive RC).

ESD protection for USB D+ / D-?

Add a TVS diode array between the USB connector and the chip: USBLC6-2SC6 (SOT-23-6) or PESD5V0L4UF. Place it within 5 mm of the USB connector pins, before any series resistors or common-mode choke. Reverse standoff voltage is 5 V (USB nominal); clamping voltage is around 12 V at 1 A. Without this, hot-plug events couple kV transients into your USB transceiver and brick the chip over time. Sometimes the USB-C controller (CH334, FE1.1S) integrates ESD; check the datasheet.

TP4056 LiPo charger circuit for a 1S 1000 mAh cell with thermistor protection.

Standard TP4056 module already does the job. Set charge current with R_PROG: I_chg (mA) = 1200 / R (kohms). For 500 mA, R_PROG = 2.4 k. For thermistor: connect a 10 k NTC across the cell, with one end to the TEMP pin and the other to GND, plus a 10 k pull-up from TEMP to 3 V output. The chip pauses charging if cell temp exceeds ~50 C or drops below ~0 C. Add a DW01 + 8205A protection IC pair downstream for over-discharge / short-circuit protection if the load can pull the cell below 2.7 V.
